concept: Node/element count as a path-topology quality target
implication: Downstream digitizing iterates the SVG node/element graph, so path topology (node count, element count, closure) is a first-class quality signal: too many nodes means a noisy over-fit trace that is slow and jittery to digitize; too few means an over-simplified edge. A node-count target bounds edge complexity independent of pixel-level metrics.

param_mapping.rationale: Node/segment count rises with path_precision, filter_speckle (low), and low splice/length thresholds. To hit a node-count target for clean topology, path_precision is the primary lever to pull DOWN once edge_iou is already satisfied, trading redundant nodes for a simpler path. Paired with a post-trace node-count check parsed from the SVG. Library states the node-iteration need directly; the numeric target is inferred (not a stated value).

hypothesis: Once edge_iou/ssim targets are met, lowering path_precision (and/or raising splice_threshold) reduces SVG node count toward a topology budget with negligible fidelity loss, producing cleaner paths for digitizing; measured by a node-count check on the output SVG.
